title: Quiz: Query Complexity
description: Test your understanding of quantum query complexity and oracle problems

questions.question: What is the quantum query complexity of unstructured search in a database of N items?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm achieves O(sqrt(N)) query complexity for unstructured search, and this is provably optimal by the adversary lower bound method.

questions.question: For Simon's problem, what is the ratio of classical to quantum query complexity?
questions.explanation: Simon's problem requires Omega(2^{n/2}) classical queries (birthday bound) but only O(n) quantum queries, demonstrating an exponential quantum speedup.

questions.question: What does the polynomial method tell us about quantum query complexity?

options.text: Upper bounds on the number of qubits needed
Lower bounds based on polynomial degree

questions.explanation: The polynomial method proves that Q(f) >= deg(f)/2, where deg(f) is the minimum polynomial degree representing f. It gives lower bounds, not upper bounds.

questions.question: A quantum algorithm making T queries can produce acceptance probabilities that are polynomials of degree at most 2T in the input bits.
questions.explanation: True. This is the foundation of the polynomial method. Each query adds at most degree 1, amplitudes are complex polynomials, and probabilities (squared amplitudes) have degree at most 2T.

questions.question: What is the quantum query complexity of computing PARITY of n bits?
questions.explanation: PARITY requires n/2 quantum queries. The polynomial degree lower bound shows Q >= n/2, and a simple algorithm achieves this. No quantum speedup over the O(n) classical bound except by constant factor.

questions.question: In the phase oracle model, how does the oracle transform the basis state |i>?
questions.explanation: The phase oracle applies: |i> -> (-1)^{x_i}|i>. This kicks back a phase of -1 if x_i = 1. This form is equivalent to the standard oracle and is often more convenient for analysis.

questions.question: What is the quantum query complexity of element distinctness (determining if all N elements are distinct)?
questions.explanation: Element distinctness has quantum query complexity Theta(N^{2/3}), achieved by Ambainis' algorithm using quantum walks. This is a polynomial speedup greater than quadratic but not exponential.

questions.question: Which problems exhibit exponential quantum query speedup? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Simon's problem, Deutsch-Jozsa, and period finding all have exponential quantum speedups. Unstructured search (Grover) only has a quadratic speedup (sqrt(N) vs N).
